items.myth: 100% coverage means the code is bug-free.
items.reality: Coverage measures *execution*, not *correctness*. Tests with weak or missing asserts can hit every line without catching anything. Mutation testing is the next-level check.
items.example: Replace every assert with `assert True` — coverage stays 100%; bug protection vanishes.

items.myth: Line coverage and branch coverage are roughly the same.
items.reality: On code with conditionals they're very different. A happy-path test can show 100% line and 50% branch. Always use branch coverage where logic branches.
items.example: `if x: return 1\nreturn 0` — test with `x=True` only: 100% line, 50% branch.

items.myth: Coverage gates are an absolute good.
items.reality: Gates help, but they're easily gamed and can punish meaningful refactors. Diff-coverage (cover what changed in the PR) is usually a better gate than total-project coverage.
items.example: A PR that deletes legacy code can *raise* coverage; gates should not punish that.

items.myth: You need to test all the code, including trivial getters and DTOs.
items.reality: Trivial code (data classes, generated code, pure framework calls) usually doesn't benefit from tests. Coverage tools should exclude them so the metric reflects *meaningful* coverage.
items.example: Generated protobuf accessors should be `omit`-ed from coverage; testing them is theatre.

items.myth: If `pytest --cov` says 0%, my tests didn't run.
items.reality: Often `--cov` is misconfigured — e.g. measuring an installed copy of the package while tests exercise a different path. Check `--cov=<correct/path>` matches what the tests import.
items.example: `pytest --cov=ragapp` measures the installed `ragapp/`; `pytest --cov=src/ragapp` measures the source tree. Pick the one your tests touch.
